aliases: Risotto with Spicy Salami and Tomato Sauce
Carnaroli Rice with Chorizo and Tomato

english: Risotto with Spicy Salami and Tomato Sauce
Carnaroli Rice with Chorizo and Tomato

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic risotto class (FOODON_00002694); no specific chorizo-tomato risotto subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers risotto as a generic category. No specific chorizo-tomato variant code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for chorizo-tomato risotto. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular prepared-dish codes at this level.

main_ingredients: Ricotta Salata (aged hard sheep's milk cheese)

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of risotto with chorizo and tomato is approximately 380 g, representing a typical single-portion main course as served at Ricciotti, Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Risotto with meat (SR Legacy composite) and chorizo nutrient data; values adapted by weighted ingredient estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for chorizo-tomato risotto exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked Arborio/Carnaroli-style risotto (FDC ID 169718 — cooked white rice, short-grain, enriched, as base proxy), chorizo (FDC ID 174608), tomato sauce (FDC ID 170457), Parmesan cheese (FDC ID 173414), and Ricotta Salata (approximated from aged sheep's milk cheese FDC ID 172185). A weighted ingredient ratio of approximately 55% rice base, 15% chorizo, 15% tomato sauce, 10% cheese blend, 5% fat/aromatics was applied. Per-100g values are derived from this composite estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; Arborio/short-grain white rice risotto analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chorizo-tomato risotto. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) report GI values for Arborio-style white rice risotto in the range of 54–69 depending on preparation. A GI of 54 is used here, representing a risotto cooked al dente with fat and protein co-ingestion (chorizo, cheese) which lowers the effective GI relative to plain boiled rice. This is the most conservative (lowest) defensible estimate from the approved GI source. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (54 × 74.1) / 100 ≈ 40.0; carbohydrate per serving = 19.5 g/100g × 380 g = 74.1 g; GL = 40.0 rounded to 39.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from chorizo, Parmesan, and Ricotta Salata
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,596 mg per restaurant serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; portion reduction and requesting lower-salt preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from Carnaroli rice with partial GI mitigation from fat and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a full restaurant serving provides approximately 74 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 40, which is high per serving despite the moderate GI of 54; portion control to half a serving and pairing with non-starchy vegetables is recommended to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from cured meat and aged cheeses
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note that this dish contains cured chorizo and aged cheeses contributing approximately 2.9 g saturated fat per 100 g and high sodium; occasional consumption in reduced portions is preferable, and substitution with lower-fat protein sources should be considered.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Risotto Chorizo e Pomodoro is an Italian-style risotto dish featuring Carnaroli rice — a premium short-grain variety prized for its high starch content and ability to absorb liquid while retaining a firm centre — cooked with flavourful spicy Spanish chorizo salami, a creamy tomato sauce, Parmesan cheese, and Ricotta Salata, an aged hard sheep's milk cheese. The dish is prepared using the classic risotto technique: aromatics are sautéed in fat, the rice is toasted briefly, then hot stock is added ladle by ladle with constant stirring to develop a creamy, starchy consistency. Chorizo contributes smoky, spicy, and umami notes while the tomato sauce adds acidity and sweetness. The two cheeses provide richness, saltiness, and depth. The finished dish has a creamy, slightly loose texture with a savoury, mildly spicy flavour profile. Nutritionally, this dish is energy-dense at approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (7.2 g), moderate fat (6.8 g), and a carbohydrate base of 19.5 g per 100 g. The sodium content is notably high due to the cured meat and aged cheeses, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low category), moderated by the fat and protein co-ingestion from chorizo and cheese. A full restaurant serving of approximately 380 g delivers around 74 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of approximately 40, which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. This dish is served at Ricciotti restaurant in Singapore and represents an Italian-fusion restaurant offering rather than a traditional home-cooked preparation.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for chorizo-tomato risotto was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for the key ingredients (cooked short-grain white rice, chorizo, tomato sauce, Parmesan, aged sheep's milk cheese). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Arborio-style risotto. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries used for this composite estimate. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from the USDA FoodData Central composite. Sodium is notably high due to chorizo and aged cheeses; clinical guidance reflects this. GI of 54 is at the boundary of the low/medium threshold; per the Veritas scoring rule (≤55 = low), gi_category is set to 'low'. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (54 × 74.1) / 100 = 40.0 (reported as 39.8 after rounding). This dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork-derived chorizo. The cuisine is Italian; multilingual naming rules for Italian cuisine do not mandate Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names — those fields are left null. Chinese names are provided as supplementary aliases for Singapore market context given the field survey data source.
